title: Barnett effect in rotating spinor dipolar quantum droplets
authors: Donghao Yan, Shaoxiong Li, Hiroki Saito
abstract: We propose releasing the spin degree of freedom to stabilize the vortex state in self-bound droplets of dipolar Bose-Einstein condensates. When a vortex is embedded into the droplet, spontaneous magnetization arises in the axial direction via a mechanism similar to the Barnett effect; that is, the orbital angular momentum is transferred to the spin angular momentum. When an external magnetic field is applied to the spontaneously magnetized droplet, the entire atomic cloud starts to rotate without changing its shape, which can be regarded as mechanical Larmor precession of a macroscopic object. A chirally different pair of droplets can form a stable bound state because of the attractive interaction between the spontaneously magnetized droplets.
summary: This paper studies how rotating vortices in dipolar Bose-Einstein condensates induce spontaneous magnetization through a mechanism similar to the Barnett effect. It shows that these droplets can undergo mechanical Larmor precession and form stable bound pairs. The study provides a complete picture of the stability and interaction of these unique, self-bound quantum objects.
main_problem: Investigating the stability, spontaneous magnetization, and interaction dynamics of rotating vortex states in self-bound spinor dipolar quantum droplets.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that rotating droplets exhibit a Barnett-like effect where orbital angular momentum transfers to spin angular momentum, creating spontaneous axial magnetization and enabling mechanical Larmor precession. They also find that chirally different droplets can form stable bound states due to magnetic attraction.
method: Numerical simulations using the extended Gross-Pita_Equation (eGPE) with Lee-Huang-Yang corrections and a variational analysis using a torus-shaped Gaussian trial wave function.
model_or_system: Spinor dipolar Bose-Einstein condensates (F=1) in free space, modeled by the eGPE including s-wave contact interaction, magnetic dipole-dipole interaction (DDI), and LHY corrections.
key_observables: Total density, spin density, orbital angular momentum, spin angular momentum, total angular momentum, magnetization, and Larmor precession frequency.
important_parameters: Dipolar strength (epsilon_dd), atom number (N), vorticity (l), and gyromagnetic ratio (gamma).
main_assumptions: The variational model assumes small polarization in the z-direction and focuses on the stability of the droplet structure rather than full spin-mixing dynamics.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows density and spin structures for non-rotating and rotating states; Fig 2 illustrates the dynamics of mechanical Larmor precession under a magnetic field ramp; Fig 4 presents stability boundaries for different vorticities; Fig 5 and 6 demonstrate robustness against noise and the dynamics of droplet pairs.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the system and the Barnett-like mechanism, presents numerical results for ground and rotating states, analyzes the dynamics of Larmor precession, provides a variational analysis for stability boundaries, and concludes with the study of droplet-pair interactions and robustness.
why_interesting: It explores a novel macroscopic manifestation of the Barnett effect in a self-bound quantum system, linking topological excitations (vortices) to spontaneous magnetization and mechanical rotation, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and the control of quantum fluids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a-c) Nonrotating ground state and (d-f) rotating (ℓ= 1) stationary state of self-bound droplets for F = 1, N = 15000, εdd = 1.2, and B = 0. (a, d) Isodensity sur- faces at half the maximum density. Arrows represent the spin vectors f(r) on the z = 0 plane, and their color shows the polarization f (r) /ρ (r). (b, e) Density (upper panels) and phase (lower panels) distributions of component m on the z = 0 plane. The size of each panel is 0.07 × 0.07. (c, f) Total density distributions on the y = 0 plane. Arrows represent the projected spin vectors fxz(r) = (fx(r), fz(r)) and their color shows |fxz(r)|/ρ(r). The size of each panel is 0.12 × 0.08. The system has translational and...

FIG. 2. Larmor precession of the vortex droplet in Figs. 1(d)- 1(f) by an external magnetic field in the y direction. (a) Time evolution of the isodensity surface observed from the +x di- rection, where By is linearly increased from 0 to 1000 during the period from t = 0 to 0.05. The arrows indicate the direc- tions of ⟨L⟩. A movie showing the time evolution is provided in the Supplemental Material [68]. (b) Time evolution of the x, y, and z components of Eq. (4). (c) Larmor frequency ωL as a function of By. The circles in the main panel are ob- tained by fitting the sinusoidal curves, as in (b). The solid line represents ωL = f⊥γBy L⊥+f⊥. The insets plot f⊥and the angle θ between ⟨L⟩and B...

FIG. 3. Bound state of two vortex droplets with ℓ= 1. (a) Isodensity surfaces at half the maximum density for the bound state. (b) Total density distributions and projection of spin vectors on the y = 0 plane. The color and length of the arrows represent fxz (r) /ρ (r) and |fxz(r)|, respectively. (c) Etotal (solid line), Eat (dashed line), and Erep (dotted line) as functions of d. The inset shows the dynamics of d followed by a slight decrease in d (a movie is provided in Supplemental Material [68]). The parameters are the same as those in Fig. 1.

FIG. 4. (a) Stability boundary of a vortex droplet with vor- ticity ℓwith respect to N and εdd for F = 1, obtained by the variational analysis. Stable vortex droplets exist in the regions above the lines. (b) Stability condition (region with circles) for vortex droplet with ℓ= 1 and F = 1, obtained by numerically solving the eGPE. Color indicates the magneti- zation ⟨fz⟩. The dashed line represents the ℓ= 1 line in (a).

FIG. 5. Stability of the vortex droplet against external distur- bances. (a) Isodensity surface at half the maximum density. (b) Density profiles on the z = 0 plane. The central hole is invisible because of the color range. The size of each panel is 0.3 × 0.3. A movie showing the time evolution is provided in the Supplemental Material [68]. The parameters are the same as those in Figs. 1(d)-1(f) in the main text. Low-pass-filtered random noise with a cutoff wave number 2π/0.01 is added to the initial state.

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The paper directly studies the transfer of orbital angular momentum to spin angular momentum, which is a fundamental mechanism for creating entanglement between external (rotation) and internal (spin) degrees of freedom.
